subject: Statistics
title: Stratified Randomization
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Stratified Randomization.

hook.text: You randomly assign 20 patients and all the elderly end up in the treatment group. Now what?

buildup.text: Stratified randomization divides participants into subgroups first, then randomizes within each subgroup.

discovery.text: Stratify by age, sex, or disease severity to ensure each group has balanced proportions of key variables.

twist.text: You can only stratify by variables you know about and measure. Unknown confounders still rely on luck.

climax.text: In small trials, stratified randomization dramatically reduces the chance of an imbalanced design.

punchline.text: Divide first, randomize second. Balance isn't left entirely to chance.

quiz.question: When is stratified randomization most useful?
options: In small trials where pure randomization might create imbalanced groups for key variables
Only in studies with thousands of participants
When you don't know any confounding variables
